fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If a type A is a proposition, then it is a set.
If a type A is contractible, then it is a proposition.
As A is contractible with some centre a:A, we have for any x,y:A identifications \chi _x \colon a = x and \chi _y\colon a = y. Therefore, we identify x with y using \chi _x^{-1} \cdot \chi _y.
If a type A is a proposition, then it is a set.
If A is a proposition, then for all x,y:A the identification type x=y is contractible.
The centre of contraction of x=y is given by our assumption that A is a proposition; the contracting identifications are obtained from the same assumption via Lemma .
If A is n+1-truncated, then for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-truncated.
By induction on n; the base case for n=-2 is given by Corollary , and the other cases are trivial.
If a type A is n-truncated, then it is also n+1-truncated.
By induction on the truncation level n, with two base cases given by Lemma and Lemma .
For any type A, the identity map 1_{A}\colon A\to A is contractible.
Immediate by identification induction.

fr:title.text: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: For any type A, the identity map 1_{A}\colon A\to A is n-truncated for all n.

We proceed by induction on the truncation level n: when n=-2, we recall that the identity map is contractible, and in the inductive step we apply the upward closure of n-truncation to each fiber of 1_{A}.
If B is a retract of A and A is a proposition, then B is a proposition.
This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.

fr:title.text: Contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If B is a retract of A and A is contractible, then B is a contractible.
Let s\colon B \to A be a section of r\colon A\to B; if a_0 is the centre of contraction of A, then the centre of contraction of B is given by ra_0. For the rest, we use Lemma and Lemma .

fr:title.text: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Any retract of an n-truncated type is n-truncated.

By induction on n; the base cases for n=-2 and n=-1 are given by Corollary and Lemma respectively. In the inductive step, we assume that s\colon B\to A exhibits B as a retract of A and that for each x,y:A the identification type x=_Ay is n+1-truncated to show that for all u,v:B the identification type u=_Bv is likewise n+1-truncated. The result follows from our assumption and the fact that u=_Bv is (by Lemma ) a retract of su=_Asv.

Fixing x,y:A we must show that \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x = y \to fx=fy is invertible. By Lemma and Lemma , it suffices to exhibit a biinverse to \mathsf {act}_{f} (it is convenient that the underlying maps of the section and retraction need not be identical).
The centre of contraction of x=y is given by our assumption that A is a proposition; the contracting identifications are obtained from the same assumption via Lemma .
By induction on the truncation level n, with two base cases given by Lemma and Lemma .
The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from Lemma and Lemma .

fr:title.text: Being a proposition is a proposition ›

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a proposition is a proposition ›

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A type A is called a set when for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is a proposition.
